item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from Getty Images and requires purchase for official use. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite not knowing SCP-1882's existence, Dr. ████████ surmised that he had been captured by enemy forces and engaged Protocol Phoebus 3.3, causing [REDACTED]. SCP-1882 switched back to the MC&D logo until regular turn-off time.

I feel like Dr. ████████ should get a commendation for that. I mean it wasn't actually them but is presumably indicative of what they would do under the circumstances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, is it Site-75 or Site-65?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I feel like it's missing something. I don't feel afraid of this thing. I want to sit down and watch it. I don't feel a terror at the back of my skull.
Just my two cents regarding the subject

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I now have a newfound respect for the foundation's researchers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the researcher managing to thwart his capture. This is interesting… does he have some device that constantly monitors the rest of the Earth for his own clones?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, but it is the full process that results in there being a corpse that is expunged. A whole lot can happen, particularly of a ritual nature as the process is implied to be a consistent one, in between alive and unharmed and a cold corpse.

Also I love the researcher short circuit addendum as well, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No. Just no. I don't see any reason to put that there, and the nature of what the SCP-1882-Bs do is pretty obvious when it mentions the corpse afterward. +1'd, but changed it to a -1 for that reason. Sorry, but unnecessary expungements really piss me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gave this a second look, reverted from -1 to +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the whole "they must be duplicates" aspect is glossed over. Who says the people on the TV are duplicates? Maybe the duplicates are the people who appear to still be alive and continuing with their lives. I'd suggest something that implies doubt about that, unless it's intended the reader, like I did, concludes that on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Counter-nitpick: I've seen multiple SCPs give a [REDACTED] after a list of known scientific effects, usually as a hint that the Foundation has access to some science we don't (usually with the implication of having useful theories about seemingly paranormal phenomena). I'd much rather have silly-sounding explanations or terminology redacted or expunged than laid out in all their B-movie glory - "psychic transmissions in the millipurple range" clunks worse than a bit of expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well done, and upvoted.

Nitpick: You shouldn't redact things in containment protocols, if possible. I think you should at least smooth that over by referencing an attached document with a list of approved materials for use if a new / temporary containment area needs to be constructed (particularly in an emergency, etc).

The rest of the expungement / redaction doesn't bother me, because I feel there's enough detail given that we can construct a reasonable and horrifying guess for the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and [REDACTED] signals.

Uuuugh. Redaction in containment protocols is like tar in honey. Originally downvoted just for this, then changed to upvote after loving the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I liked all the [DATA EXPUNGED]'s, kept it nice and creepy. What you can imagine from vague hints is almost always worse than knowing what really happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is my biggest gripe with the article. Not enough to not upvote since I really like some other parts (the researcher), but it's definitely a flaw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, you can get horrible gang rape from any ol' media outlet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and proceed to [DATA EXPUNGED]

This actually bothers me because it takes what could be interesting and took that information away, so I'm left with a big nothing. I'm gonna assume it's horrible gang rape and upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well written and quite creepy. I was thinking alternate universe, but clones work as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather liked the bit where the Researcher was duplicated and managed to short-circuit the timeline by using Foundation protocols. Not enough for them to survive, but enough to just ruin that broadcast.

Me too!

I upvoted but this feels a lot like the normalpornfornormalpeople creepy pasta that was linked in the forums last week

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noted the signalling system, but in the containment procedures, it explicitly states that any attempts to open the case should be done in a fashion untraceable to the Foundation. So, why can it not be opened once moved to a safe location using safe resources, at least for a very limited amount of time? If Zaratrustra's reply above is correct, and it will actually explode once tampered with, that should probably be mentioned.

A line giving a brief description of what was found the first time the case was opened does seem like the better option though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because each and every day, you get to see a new, random, and possibly famous person at their most vulnerable and intimate for about a whole day before getting [DATA EXPUNGED]. What's not to like?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Similarly, an attempt to open SCP-1882's casing led to it emitting a microwave pulse - probably a signalling system.

Right there. Letting people know the location of your super-secret facility is reason enough not to open it. Though since they had to do it at least once to find out that happened, maybe just throw in a line about how the insides are standard for a television of that make and model.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The TV is not magic. Well, aside from running without power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is that if someone starts tampering with it, it'll either send a warning signal to its maker, or explode and be useless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This didn't creep me out until we got those last little details suggesting what 1882-B might be

See, I ended up with just the opposite; the closest I got is maybe some kind of quasi-vampire/zombie thing going on. Werewolves perhaps? Those Japanese ghost-demon-things from the Grudge?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather liked the bit where the Researcher was duplicated and managed to short-circuit the timeline by using Foundation protocols. Not enough for them to survive, but enough to just ruin that broadcast. I also like it that the individuals on the broadcast aren't actually removed from the rest of the world but just duplicated. Adds a nice bit of existential doubt. ("Am I real or am I just a temporary duplicate? Does my existence end when the TV is shut off?")

I do agree that this would be better if there was some better reason for not disassembling this. Even something like they actually recovered 3 intact copies (in addition to the destroyed ones) and when they tried to disassemble one, it fried its own internals by a boobytrap. I'm not sure which would be creepier: the remaining 2 display the same broadcast (implying that all of them show the same thing), or they show different broadcasts.

As for the MCD thing… I don't see why they would be providing this to their customers (remember: their customer base are ultra-rich, ultra-bored individuals looking for "that something special"). As it is, it's not Factory: there's neither an obviously useful aspect of this nor a cost to the user. And it's definitely not Wondertainment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for obfuscating what exactly SCP-1882-B are. I initially thought you'd completely missed it out until I saw the bit at the end, but my hopes were dashed with a clumsy [DATA EXPUNGED].

That, and I'm a bit confused by MC&D's involvement. To my knowledge, MC&D don't make SCPs, they auction them (this seems more of a Factory job). And I can't see what value this item represents if they did make it. Who is going to buy this thing, in all honesty? You could just buy a bunch of horror movies if you liked the horror and violence, without paying through the nose to buy an anomalous TV set. Seems more like this is using MC&D to prop up the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This didn't creep me out until we got those last little details suggesting what 1882-B might be. This does a fantastic job of hinting at the horrific, but not actually painting a complete picture. I really liked it, once I read the whole thing.

I have two questions / concerns:

Why would you buy such a thing from MC&D? What does it do for you other than "you get to see these horrific things?" Are the numbers the reward and watching this event is the price? Or are these just generic boring ol' sadists? I think there's a way to suggest what it does for you while still leaving how it works and why in mystery.

And what other stuff must the TV be insulated from? This is just kind of basic, but I think it's frowned on to have [REDACTED] in containment procedures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How exactly does it form the duplicates? It seems like there would have to be some kind of selection process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to know why the casing can't be opened. Is it remarkably durable, or have the attempts been delicate to avoid damaging the object?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I like this one. Actually winced when you mentioned the tiger, and my upvote was secured when you said that they were "formerly human". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We already have multiple magic TVs and objects that randomly kidnap people. But it's not a bad article.

No vote for now.
